coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded polyoxomolybdate frameworks toward high-performance supercapacitor

coredata.dc:description: In the pursuit of high energy supercapacitor electrodes, we have engineered an exceptional pseudocapacitive material with phenomenal energy density and cycle stability based on lemon-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polyoxomolybdate (H3PMo12O40, POMo). Herein, we demonstrate that the CQDs and nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) can be employed as effective nano-enhancers to surge up the electrochemical supercapacitive performances of POMo. These QDs were developed by the hydrothermal carbonization of pulp free lemon juice. POMo, a metal-inorganic framework of Mo and O with P at the center, was synthesized via simple precipitation method from (NH4)2MoO4 and NH4H2PO4 in acidic condition. The binary composites of POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs were prepared via simply dispersing them under ultrasonication. All of the materials were characterized using state-of-the-art techniques for molecular and electrochemical analysis. The as-prepared composites delivered a specific capacitance (C sp) of 660 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1 for POMo-10 % CQDs, whereas an exceptionally high C sp of 1960 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 for POMo-10 % N-CQDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 (aq) which are ~35 and ~120 times higher than that of the pristine POMo, respectively. The N-CQDs based composites have superior charging-discharging tolerance with nearly 96 % initial C sp-retention after 3000 cycles at 20.0 A g−1. These QDs leverage the development of comprehensive conductive networks within the POMo matrix, allowing facile charge transfer and ion migration throughout POMo with substantial kinetics enhancement. This, in turn, facilitates the transport and storage of electrolyte ions within the intricate and deeply branched micropores. The novel electrode materials based on polyoxometalates and lemon derived oxygen-enriched nitrogen-doped CQDs presented in this study opens up new possibilities for improving capacitive and rate performances in high energy supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded polyoxomolybdate frameworks toward high-performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Dhaka, Dhaka 1000, Bangladesh
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Dhaka, Dhaka-1000, Bangladesh

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the pursuit of high energy supercapacitor electrodes, we have engineered an exceptional pseudocapacitive material with phenomenal energy density and cycle stability based on lemon-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polyoxomolybdate (H3PMo12O40, POMo). Herein, we demonstrate that the CQDs and nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) can be employed as effective nano-enhancers to surge up the electrochemical supercapacitive performances of POMo. These QDs were developed by the hydrothermal carbonization of pulp free lemon juice. POMo, a metal-inorganic framework of Mo and O with P at the center, was synthesized via simple precipitation method from (NH4)2MoO4 and NH4H2PO4 in acidic condition. The binary composites of POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs were prepared via simply dispersing them under ultrasonication. All of the materials were characterized using state-of-the-art techniques for molecular and electrochemical analysis. The as-prepared composites delivered a specific capacitance (C sp) of 660 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1 for POMo-10 % CQDs, whereas an exceptionally high C sp of 1960 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 for POMo-10 % N-CQDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 (aq) which are ~35 and ~120 times higher than that of the pristine POMo, respectively. The N-CQDs based composites have superior charging-discharging tolerance with nearly 96 % initial C sp-retention after 3000 cycles at 20.0 A g−1. These QDs leverage the development of comprehensive conductive networks within the POMo matrix, allowing facile charge transfer and ion migration throughout POMo with substantial kinetics enhancement. This, in turn, facilitates the transport and storage of electrolyte ions within the intricate and deeply branched micropores. The novel electrode materials based on polyoxometalates and lemon derived oxygen-enriched nitrogen-doped CQDs presented in this study opens up new possibilities for improving capacitive and rate performances in high energy supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Concerns about global energy usage have emerged as a result of rising worldwide population. The dwindling supply of fossil fuels and the effects of climate change necessitate a shift in society's reliance on renewable and sustainable energy sources [1,2]. Solar and wind power production technologies are currently very much appreciated for producing sustainable energy [2]. However, the usage of electrical energy storage devices is equally crucial due to the intermittent and unexpected nature of these power producing technologies [3]. To advance toward a civilization that only depends on sustainable and renewable energy sources, new electrical energy storage devices must be introduced that have better energy and power densities, higher rate capabilities with, rapid charging/discharging, durable cycle lifespan inexpensive and environmentally safe [3,4]. Energy storage technologies have undergone drastic alterations in the last two decades. At the turn of this century, Li-ion batteries consolidated as a reliable technology that fueled the development of a wide range of portable consumer electronics. The main driving forces behind this convergence have been increased energy densities and design flexibility [5]. However, the coming of the new century has resulted in a shift in focus and a proliferation of new demands for energy storage technologies. In fact, the trend toward incorporating storage into a new, sustainable energy model is being strengthened with time and has given rise to fresh, insistent demands [3,5,6].

Electrochemical capacitors also famous as supercapacitors have lately mustered considerable attention as one the most leading energy storage devices [7,8]. Focus of scientists all around the globe have concentrated on supercapacitors due to their captivating attributes such as prompt dynamic response, release of burst-energy within milliseconds, i.e., elevated power density, longer-faster charging/discharging cycles, etc., filling the existing gap between traditional capacitors and Li-ion battery technologies [9]. Supercapacitors operate through two mechanistic processes [10]: electric double-layer capacitance [11] and pseudocapacitance [12]. The former functions via electrostatic charge separation occurring at the electrode/electrolyte interface, whereas the later stores energy performing reversible redox reactions without concomitant phase transition [9–12]. Although supercapacitors can generate higher power densities, their restricted energy density limits them to being just complementary electrical components for battery operations. Recent research in this area has focused on increasing energy density while retaining their other compelling criteria such as power density and cycling life [7–9]. Pseudocapacitive metal oxides have been intensively studied in this regard [13]. These investigations achieved significantly higher specific capacitance (C sp) than electrical double-layer capacitors made of carbon-based materials [13,14].

Polyoxometalates (POMs) [15–17], first identified in 1826 by Berzelius, are nanosized transition metal‑oxygen clusters combining oxygen with early transition metals (e.g., Mo, W, V, Nb, etc.) at their maximum oxidation states have intriguing pseudocapacitive charge storage properties [17,18]. POMs may contain hundreds of metal atoms, as much as nearly of 368 metal atoms, that accomplish nuclearities in a just single molecular cluster [19]. They possess greater lattice oxygen count per POM molecule compared to other metal oxides, making them exceptional ion reservoirs with ample charge storage sites. POMs behave as electron sponges and can also absorb protons (H+) in acidic media showcasing tremendous protonic conductivity with supreme acid tolerance. They have a robust structure enduring rapid, reversible, multi-electron redox reactions with negligible structural alterations [17–19]. However. inferior surface area along with poor electrical conductivity render POMs as an ineffective material for proper charge storage application [19,20]. Musa et al. [20] have impregnated rGO with POM moieties (phosphotungstic acid, PTA) boosting the electrical conductivity and surface area of PTA alleviating the restacking phenomenon of the graphene nanosheets as well. The resulted composite with only 3 % PTA loading with rGO exhibited a C sp of 123 F g−1 where the C sp values of PTA and rGO are 13 and 51 F g−1, respectively. Similar, improvements have been observed for AC/POMo [21,22], pinecone AC/POMo [23], rGO/Co-POM [24], rGO-PW12 [24], 2D GNs/POM [25], GO/POM [26–29], CNT/POM [30,31] etc., composites. The mediocre performance of POMs can further be boosted with the incorporation of other metal oxides within their matrix. In our recent work, we have successfully boosted up the C sp of aluminum-based POMs by seven times with the addition of only 5 % MnO2 in the composite. However, the composite eventually fails due to the lack of electrical conductance [32]. Another issue with POMs is their high solubility in aquatic medium [18,23,27]. Moreover, POM-moieties agglomerate with one another limiting their full potential by concealing their active sites for charge storage [17,18,20,26,30]. So, to prevent their aquatic dissolution and agglomeration along with improving their conductivity and surface area, we need to anchor them with conducting substrates like conducting polymers [33] (polypyrrole, polyaniline), carbon nanotubes [30,31], graphene [24–29], or activated carbons [21–23].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [34] and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [35] have demonstrated top-tier outcomes in the improvement of supercapacitors. Quantum dots (QDs) are zero dimensional, quasi-spherical, fluorescent nanoparticles having dimensions <10 nm [34]. CQDs have gained recognition as novel materials for environmental and energy related applications due to their environmental benignity, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, availability, facile synthesis, chemical stability, and low cost [36,37]. Strategical tunning of size and surface functionalization of CQDs, by controlling several reaction parameters such as pH, temperature, starting materials and so on, can bring about modifications in optical and electrical properties [34,36–38]. The size and shape of QDs have significant effects over their functionality in applications like light emission, electrical conductivity, and catalyzing reactions [34,35]. Their size dictates their optical and electronic attributes, i.e., smaller QDs frequently demonstrate improved electrical conductivity and tunable emission wavelengths as a result of quantum events [34,36,42]. In addition, their shape affects surface area, revealing several catalytic sites and influencing how well they function in reactions. Understanding such factors enables accurate manipulation of QDs characteristics, enhancing their efficacy in LEDs, sensors, supercapacitors, conductive materials, and catalysts for a variety of uses, from sophisticated catalytic procedures to energy-efficient light emission to electrochemical charge storage [34–36,40,42,48]. Nevertheless, their supercapacitive trends do not meet the desired standard, as they suffer from a considerable drawback in terms of energy density. Blending them with other pseudocapacitive materials can improve their performances [37,38]. Wei et al. have designed CQDs/Co3O4 composite that delivered a C sp of 1603 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 [36]. Microwave thermolysis synthesized CQDs decorated NiCo2O4 nanowires demonstrated a C sp of 2202 F g−1, which is 30 times higher than pristine NiCo2O4 [37]. N-doping further reinforce the performance of CQDs. The introduction of N-CQDs into the matrix of Bi2O3 (C sp = 849 F g−1), transforms its morphology from being porous nanosheets to hollow micropores manifesting a high C sp of 1775 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 [38]. N-CQDs anchored NiO/Co3O4 ternary composite displayed a C sp of 1046 F g−1 having 95.7 % retention after 10,000 cycles at 15.0 A g−1 [39].

In this work, we have investigated the charge storage trends in the binary composites of polyoxomolybdate (H3PMo12O40, POMo) with CQDs and N-CQDs. Both CQDs and N-CQDs have been synthesized from lemon juice via hydrothermal carbonization. Lemon juice is cost-effective, readily available, non-toxic, biocompatible, pure and consistent in composition, eco-friendly and sustainable [40]. Citric acid, which is one of the major constituents of lemon juice, has a molecular structure that is known to facilitate efficient carbonization at a low carbonization temperature [46]. Moreover, lemon juice contains ascorbic acid (vitamin C), a natural reducing agent, that assists the reduction and stabilization of QDs during the synthesis process, leading to a better control of size and surface functionalization [40,46]. These features of lemon juice simplify the overall synthesis process, enhances the optical properties of QDs, aligns with the principles of green chemistry producing minimum wastes, helps in achieving reproducible results and high-quality QDs with fewer impurities [40,46]. Nitrogen doping was carried out with ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), which is a good source of both nitrogen and oxygen, hence nitrogen-doped oxygen-enriched CQDs have been achieved. Both of the QDs have been found to be highly functionalized with several oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups. High degree of functionalization increases the wettability enabling better electrode/electrolyte contact and also induces pseudocapacitance to some extent [41]. The inclusion of CQDs and N-CQDs in the matrix of POMo generates a porous, defected structure with ion transporting tunnels enabling better ion permeation with better surface area. Thus, CQDs and N-CQDs make the inner surface of POMo available for charge storage. Moreover, their presence allows ionic migration into the deep branches and pores of POMo, attributable to their conductive nature [34–36]. Furthermore, they tend to hold the structure of POMo together toughening their structure against dissolution and agglomeration, hence the binary composites exhibit prolonged cycle lifespan [42]. For all these reasons, their binary composites, especially POMo-10 % CQDs and POMo-10 % N-CQDs, outshine in their electrochemical charge accumulation performances. In this study, we have achieved a C sp of 1960 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 with 96 % retention of initial capacitance at an ultra-high current density of 20.0 A g−1. Moreover, enormous energy and power densities of 225 W h kg−1 and 475 W kg−1, respectively, has been achieved with a C sp of 1796 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1, which unequivocally demonstrates the immense potential applicability of our materials in the realm of high-performance supercapacitor electrodes.

The analytical grade chemicals and materials such as ammonium molybdate, (NH4)2MoO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, Japan), ammonium dihydrogen phosphate, NH4H2PO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, Japan), potassium chloride, KCl (Sigma, Germany), potassium bromide, KBr (Sigma, Germany), absolute ethanol, CH3-CH2-OH (Merck, Germany), acetone (CH3-CO-CH3), hydrogen peroxide, H2O2 (Scharlau, Spain), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, EDTA (Sigma, Germany), graphite rod (diameter: 6.15 mm, purity: 99.99 %) as working electrode (Alfa Aeser, USA), poly(vinylidene fluoride), PVDF (Alfa Aesar, USA), de-ionized (DI) water (conductivity: 0.055 μS cm−1 at 25.0 °C) from HPLC grade water purification systems (BOECO, BOE 8082060, Germany), sulfuric acid, H2SO4 (Merck, Germany), hydrochloric acid, HCl (RCI, Thailand), were used without further purification.

Lemon juice, a popular and affordable beverage, comprises of the sugars sucrose, glucose, fructose, citric acid, and ascorbic acid required for the effective production of carbon-based quantum dots. The main components of the lemon juice were hydrothermally carbonized to create quantum dots [40,43]. Polyoxomolybdate (H3PMo12O40) was synthesized from ammonium molybdate with the aid of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate and hydrochloric acid [44,45]. The detailed synthetic protocols of the materials studied are described in supporting information (SI, see Schemes S1 and S2). Brief descriptions of the synthetic methods are given below.

Pulp-free lemon juice and absolute ethanol were mixed at ratio of 2:1 thoroughly to create a translucent solution. 2.0 mL H2O2 was then added in the mixture, stirred magnetically for about 1.0 h to ensure its complete consumption. Then, the mixture was placed in a 100.0 mL Teflon autoclave vessel and was firmly closed. The autoclave was held at 200 °C for 24.0 h in a muffle furnace. The autoclave was opened after cooling to room temperature and a brownish-yellow suspension of CQDs was produced. The suspension was centrifuged to separate and collect the black residues and were kept for further use [43,46,47]. Same procedure was followed to synthesize N-CQDs without the use of absolute ethanol and H2O2. The lemon juice was then carbonized at 200 °C for 3.0 h wherein dopant EDTA was additionally added as the source of elemental nitrogen (N) [40,48] (see in Scheme S2). All of the composites of POMo and QDs were prepared through the solution dispersion method. Different materials with expected compositions, as noted in Table 1 , were dispersed in absolute ethanol. Then the mixture was ultrasonicated for 1.0 h. Finally, the absolute ethanol was slowly evaporated at 90 °C to collect the as-formed composite materials [32] (further in Scheme S3).

Ammonium molybdate was dissolved in dilute ammonium dihydrogen phosphate solution. To establish homogeneity, the mixture was agitated fleetly for 30 min. It was then acidified with concentrated HCl (2.0 M). HCl was introduced dropwise until the formation of yellow precipitate, confirming the successful synthesis of POMo. It was then filtered, washed with pure alcohol, and dried in vacuum dry [44,45].

All the prepared materials were characterized using several state-of-art techniques including FT-IR (Frontier FT-NIR/MIR, Perkin Elmer, USA), XRD (PANalytical X'Pert Pro diffractometer), UV–Vis spectroscopy (UV-1650A, Shimadzu, Japan), TEM and SAED (JEM-2100F operated at 200 kV FE (Field Emission) accelerating voltage; Tokyo, Japan), SEM and EDS (ZEISS Sigma 300, Hitachi S3400N, Germany) and TGA (Hitachi TG/DTA 7200, Japan).

A computer controlled electrochemical analyzer (Model: 760E, CH Instruments, USA) was employed to evaluate the supercapacitive behavior through cyclic voltammetry, chronopotentiometry or galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. Three electrodes in a single compartment were employed for the electrochemical measurements consisting of a working electrode (WE), a counter electrode (CE), and a reference electrode (RE). Here, the WE is a graphite electrode, a spiral platinum wire (Pt) with high surface area is used as the CE and Ag/AgCl/KCl (sat.) electrode is used as the RE. All the measurements were carried out in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytic solution. The graphite electrodes (diameter: 6.15 mm, purity: 99.99 %) were chemically modified manually with 95 wt% materials mixed with 5 wt% PVDF binder in a solvent of absolute ethanol (1000 μL). The active mass of the fabricated electrode materials was in the range of micrograms (~10−6 g). Performance parameters were calculated using the following equations [20,42].
(1)

I × ∆ t

(2)

where C sp is the specific capacitance, I (A) is the current, Δt (s) is the discharging time, ΔV (V) is the operating potential window, m (g) is the active mass of the fabricated material, E (W h kg−1) and P (W kg−1) are energy density and power density, respectively.
All the prepared samples were subjected to different elemental, morphological, crystallographic, thermal and electrochemical analyses.

The FTIR spectra of all the pure materials and their composites were recorded at ambient conditions as represented in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1A exhibits the FTIR spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs. At the first sight, these materials are highly functionalized with several oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups. As assigned on the spectra, these groups include CO, CN, OH, NH, CH, CC, COC and so on [40,41,43,46], i.e., their outer peripheries are ornamented with these functional groups (see in inset). The occurrence of bands for CN, CNH bonds in N-CQDs implies that nitrogen atoms were successfully incorporated into the graphitic aromatic rings [40,41]. Moreover, lower segments of the spectrum below 1200 cm−1 hints the presence of CH, CN (in aromatic ring) and CO, COO bonds [41,43,47,48]. Similar results were obtained in the studies of Sahoo et al. [43] with CQDs. A highly functionalized surface with various organic-based functional groups were observed with CH, CO, COC, and CO at 2992, 1732, 1201, and 1021 cm−1. Similar broad overlapping due to strong stretching vibration of NH and OH in the range of 2900–3600 cm−1 for N-CQDs were found by Tadesse et al. [40]. Furthermore, presence of several O, N-based functional groups were also evident by the FTIR analysis.

Fig. 1B demonstrates the FTIR spectra of POMo and its composites with CQDs and N-CQDs. The four characteristic bands of POMo can be seen in the range of 750–1100 cm−1 [18,20,44,49,50]. PO bond of the central PO4 tetrahedron and MoO bond of MoO6 octahedron give stretching vibrations at 1060 and 963 cm−1, respectively [44]. In addition, there are two types of bridging bond present in POMo namely corner sharing oxygen (Mo-Oc-Mo) and edge sharing oxygen (Mo-Oe-Mo) [50]. Their presence is confirmed by the stretching vibrations occurring at 874 and 790 cm−1, respectively [44,49].

Zhang et al. [49] have reported identical FTIR results for Keggin-POMo. These bands confirm the successful synthesis of POMo with Keggin structure [49,50]. A relatively weak band corresponding to PO can be found at around 506 cm−1 [51,52]. On other hand, Keggin POMo retains its originality in POMo-5 % CQDs, while it underwent some chemical changes in POMo-5 % N-CQDs indicated by the rectangles. This showcases that not only simple physical mixing is at play for POMo and N-CQDs, a chemical combination is responsible for these kinds of behavior in FTIR spectra. Oxygen-enriched carbon substrate permit better contact with the aqueous electrolyte promoting improved wettability and permeation of ions even into the deep branches of the material. Furthermore, these oxygen-bearing groups can also facilitate pseudocapacitive contribution in charge storage via rapid reversible redox reactions [41].

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) investigations were done to anticipate the crystallographic microstructures of all the materials. The related diffractograms are provided in Fig. 1C and D. In cases of CQDs and N-CQDs, a broad peak at 2θ = ~21° is found to occur for the existence of graphitized carbon basal plane (002) [35,37,40,42,53–56]. Roomi et al. [53] have witnessed a noisy and weak broad peak at 2θ = 21° for CQDs. Tadesse et al. [40] also found graphitic carbon basal plane (002) for N-CQDs at 2θ = 23° almost close to that of this work. Both of them are amorphous in character, as seen from their broad and less intense peaks [37,40]. In comparison to CQDs, N-CQDs exhibit a more dispersive diffractogram implying that the matrix of N-CQDs is more electron rich and disordered as expected from nitrogen doping [55,56]. The d-spacing or interplanar spacing of CQDs and N-CQDs are 0.41 and 0.46 nm, respectively. Xu et al. [57] have found the d-spacing to be 0.402 nm for CQDs, which is close to the obtained value. Qiang et al. [58] have observed a broader peak, depicting amorphous nature, occurring at 20.5° attributable to the graphitized carbon basal plane (002) that demonstrate an interplanar spacing of 0.43 nm, close to that observed in this study. Both of the interplanar spacings of CQDs and N-CQDs are broader compared to graphite (0.34 nm). This is a clear indication of rise in the amorphous part caused due to the existence of oxygen- and nitrogen-bearing functional groups at their periphery [58]. Furthermore, the large interplanar spacing of N-CQDs compared to CQDs indicates that former is more amorphous in nature attributable to its nitrogen doped nature [57,58]. So, due to its amorphous nature and presence of both oxygen- and nitrogen-based groups, N-CQDs are expected to be better performing in terms of charge storage with respect to CQDs [41]. XRD patterns (Fig. 1D) of POMo and its composites unveil that all of them are highly crystalline as the peaks are very sharp and narrow. The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 10.7, 15.2, 21.5, 26.4, 30.6, and 36.1° confirm the successful synthesis of Keggin POMo with body-centered cubic structure [18,20,59,60]. Diffraction patterns of composites demonstrate striking similarities to that of POMo, though a minor shift toward higher 2θ values were seen (inset), signifying that POMo retains its crystallinity in the composites with little or no change just as shown with FTIR analysis. The characteristics peaks of CQDs and N-CQDs in the composites are too weak to be discerned due to their low content [37]. The Scherrer's equation Eq. (4) establishes a relationship between the crystallite size of a solid with the broadening of a peak in a diffraction pattern [59].
(4)
D =
0.9
λ
β
cos θ
where D (nm) is the crystallite size, β is full width at half maximum (FWHM in radian) of a peak, θ (rad) is the angle, and λ (nm) is the wavelength (0.154 nm) of the incident X-rays. Crystallite sizes of our studied materials were determined using Eq. (4) and XRD data from Fig. 1C and D presented in Table 1.

Presence of nitrogen is attributable to the enhanced crystallite size of N-CQDs (0.73 nm) compared to CQDs (0.25 nm) previously stated from the interplanar spacing analysis. A nitrogen doped structure has greater crystallite sizes than any undoped material [61]. The replacement of C atoms with N atoms due to doping results in tensile strain that may lead to the expansion of crystallite size. Moreover, lattice distortion due to doping and defects, may add to this phenomenon [61]. The presence of CQDs and N-CQDs creates new scope for hydrogen bonding [62]. Crystallite sizes of POMo and its composites are almost similar but the composites of POMo//N-CQDs are slightly larger than the composites of POMo//CQDs. This will enable better ion permeation and migration within the inner branches of POMo//N-CQDs promoting greater charge storage. During the ex-situ preparation of the composites, aggregation of particles to some extent may have occurred due to hydrogen bonding. POMo generally showcases significant structural organization due to their stable and symmetrical molecular structures [15–18,20,26]. But, as soon as they are mixed and ultrasonicated with highly functionalized CQDs and N-CQDs, as inferred from FTIR analysis in Fig. 1A, hydrogen bonding [62] between the oxygen- and nitrogen-bearing functional groups (OH, NH, CO) and the oxygen atoms present in POMo can lead to molecular aggregation [40,41,43,46]. This aggregation, resulted from networked hydrogen bonding, can influence the functionalities of the composites in optical properties, catalytic activity, stability, electrical conductivity and supercapacitive properties [50–52]. To restrain aggregation while retaining the quality of the composites, several strategies such as surface modification of QDs with sterically hindering groups (e.g., polyethylene glycol, long alkyl chains), controlled functionalization, use of surfactants or stabilizers, appropriate solvent selection, pH and ionic strength control, and use of cross-linking agents can be employed [19,21,25–27,59].

QDs are very luminous [34,40,41,46–48,57,58]. Owing to the onset of quantum confinement effect at micro-molecular scales, they have wider and adjustable band gap energy (E g) [34]. Carbon-based QDs are fluorophores in a sense that they absorb photons and then promptly fluoresce longer-wavelength photons. Both CQDs and N-CQDs are fluorescent particles [40,41,47,48]. Thus, they were analyzed employing UV–Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 2A, B illustrates the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs, respectively.

It can be noticed that both of them absorb substantially and display band in the UV region. The prominent absorption peak in Fig. 2A centered at 342 nm may be attributed to the (π → π*) transition of C=C group [43,46,47], whereas the aromatic ring's CC (π → π*) and the CO/CN group's (n → π*) transitions cause the two distinct absorption peaks of N-CQDs in Fig. 2B, which are located at 322 and 366 nm, respectively [40,41,48]. Wang et al. [63] have investigated the optical properties of N-CQDs, where they also found two absorption peaks for N-CQDs originating from CC (π → π*) and CO/CN (n → π*) transitions. This can be inferred that the presence of amino (NH2) groups, that function as auxochromes [64], causes the absorption peaks to blue shift (from 342 nm for CQDs to 322 nm for N-CQDs) following N-doping. Both of them glow brightly under UV-light as illustrated in Fig. 2C. CQDs and N-CQDs absorb in the UV area and emit green light at 539 and 520 nm, respectively. Just like these, green emitting CQDs were developed by Xu et al. [65] from tartaric acid and Bran via one-step solvothermal approach. This material exhibited almost identical optical characteristics compared with the lemon-derived QDs with an absorption peak at 350 nm of this study.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to examine the thermal stabilities of all the composites and pure samples across the temperature range of 25–1000 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in nitrogen environment. Fig. 2D displays the TG curves of CQDs, N-CQDs, POMo, POMo-5 % CQDs and POMo-5 % N-CQDs, respectively. The initial decline in the curve POMo is due to the loss of adsorbed water molecules. POMo, being an inorganic molecule, is very stable till 800 °C. After 800 °C, the observed significant mass loss may be attributable to the ensuing breakdown process [18,59,66].
(5)

· n

Its composites are incredibly thermally stable too. They imitate breakdown process of POMo. Material retention for POMo, POMo-5 % CQDs, and POMo-5 % N-CQDs at 1000 °C is 17, 30, and 43 %, respectively. Thermal tolerance of POMo become higher upon the addition of 5 % CQDs in its matrix. Moreover thermal tolerance is further improved when 5 % N-CQDs is introduced in the POMo matrix. From this we can infer that the addition of CQDs and N-CQDs improves the crystallinity of POMo, and hence the improvement of thermal stability [67]. Additional stability imparted by N-CQDs may be due to the formation of H-bonds between POMo and N-CQDs [62].

CQDs are thermally more stable than N-CQDs. It is speculated that structural defects existed in a material function as weak links and deteriorate its thermal stability [67,68]. As mentioned earlier, nitrogen doping introduces more defects in the crystal lattice along with more pores and structural distortions in N-CQDs making it more vulnerable to thermal treatment [56,61,68]. Therefore, it may be remarked that N-CQDs possess an inferior crystalline structure, making them more prone to thermal degradation. Arjmand et al. [68] studied the thermal properties of N-doped and undoped carbon nanotubes (CNTs). N-doping render the materials with more internal defects making them more susceptible to thermal degradation. This reveals the poor thermal tolerance of nitrogen doped materials. Hence, N-CQDs are more defective and amorphous compared to CQDs as previously inferred from XRD analyses.

TEM images of CQDs are presented in Fig. 3A. Upon magnification to 20 nm scale, at different color contrast, carbogenic dots can be seen clearly. Dots are nearly uniform, spherical in size, and are distributed homogeneously throughout the magnified region. Here, particle size of CQDs typically lie in the range of 3–6 nm (Fig. 3B). The average diameter of the dots is found to be approximately 4.9 nm. Xu et al. [65] synthesized highly fluorescent CQDs that have an average diameter of 4.85 nm revealed from their TEM images. Qiang et al. [58] have synthesized CQDs from potato starch having lateral size in the range of 2–5 nm with a mean diameter <5 nm. Diffused rings in SAED patterns (Fig. 3C) certifies the amorphous nature of CQDs, which has been previously predicted from the XRD data. Surface topographical representation in Fig. 3D reveals the position and density of dots present in the sample. The spikes, in this representation, indicate the position of dots. Further morphological and topographical analysis were done with SEM images.

As projected from XRD analysis, POMo were found to be highly crystalline. At the micro-level, POMo particles have big block like morphology with sharp, well-formed edges and boundaries. When zoomed into nano-level, it is observed that the material is porous and structured into spherical aggregates ranging from 8 to 10 nm. The surface of POMo is devoid of any cracks. SEM images of CQDs and N-CQDs are of very similar type. Both of them have structural cracks and are porous having tunnel like structure. These cracks may be formed due to the hydrothermal treatment [63,64]. The surface-crack density for CQDs is higher compared to N-CQDs. However, the widths of cracks are different as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The mean surface cracks of N-CQDs (15–17 nm) are wider compared to that of CQDs (8–10 nm). Hence, it is expected to be less thermally stable than CQDs, which is also demonstrated in the TGA analysis (Fig. 2D). These cracks or tunnels can act as effective reservoirs of ions during the charging period of the supercapacitor [69].

Their presence imparts the materials with conductive channels and connected networks that can support efficient permeation of ions enabling superior supercapacitive performances [19,21–24]. Having wider tunnel gaps, N-CQDs can provide more active sites for charge storage than CQDs, and hence expected to have higher C sp [36,38,41]. CQDs have a layered structure, whereas N-CQDs are more or less flat can be seen at 1000 nm magnification. All of these differences between CQDs and N-CQDs may stem from N-doping. Surface topography of POMo, CQDs and N-CQDs are presented in Fig. 5 that unfolds their surface roughness. The surface roughness of CQDs is much more pronounced than N-CQDs and of POMo is moderate.

Fig. 6C confirms the existence of Mo, P and O in POMo with 12.5, 4.4 and 83.2 % composition, respectively. The % composition is more or less consistent with its molecular formula, H3PMo12O40. The binary composite, POM-5 % N-CQDs, was also subjected to EDS analysis. Its EDS spectrum in Fig. 6D portraits the occurrence of C, N along with Mo, O and P. This indicates the successful formation of the composite. POM has an extremely low electrical conductivity. This is the prime reason that restricts their utilization as an electrode material for supercapacitor application [15,17,18,20,22–24]. Moreover, their surface area is low which diminishes the availability of the redox active sites that can participate in charge storage [15,18,20]. The incorporation of N-CQDs with POMo introduces C and N in the matrix. Their existence, especially of C, endow the matrix of POMo with superb electrical conductivity generating a 3D conductive network all over the material [21–26] while N mainly contributes via pseudocapacitacne [35,38].

If we look closely at the inset SEM images in Fig. 6C and D, we can observe that the surface area has enormously increased from POMo to POMo-5 % N-CQDs. Small aggregates of particles having spherical shape joins together to generate the porous micrometric framework of POMo-5 % N-CQDs. This leads to the full exposure of redox active sites of POMo in the composite for pseudocapacitance that were not available before in the pristine POMo. So, we can conclude that the presence of N-CQDs benefits POMo by increasing the surface area enormously unveiling active sites for pseudocapacitance. The same thing is expected for the composites of POMo and CQDs. Thus, we can anticipate that the binary composites of POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs will have monumental values of C sp as compared to the constituent pristine materials.

The colossal increment of surface area with the inclusion of QDs in the matrix of POMo is further evident by the SEM micrographs presented in Fig. 7 . There have been several distinguishable and perceptible alterations with the inclusion of N-CQDs to the POMo matrix, which are also true for the inclusion of CQDs. Porosity is increased tremendously than previously with concomitant increase in effective surface area. Inner surfaces are now exposed and ready for intercalation and redox pseudocapacitance [69]. More redox active sites, situated both on inner and outer perimeter, can participate in charge storage. Thus, ions can penetrate deep into the material accessing the inner branches that were previously unavailable. So, better permeation of charges is possible at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces [41–43]. Furthermore, N-CQDs residing on POMo allows improved electronic/electrolytic conduction with the emergence of 3D integrated conductive network. Hence, more ion transporting channels are now available for effective charge transportation [34–39,41,43]. Therefore, the composites of POMo with CQDs or N-CQDs should have better C sp than pristine POMo as well as higher energy and power densities.

Purushothaman et al. [69] have engineered hydrothermally produced nanosheet-assembled NiO microstructures. The NiO nanosheets synthesized at 160 °C played a significant role showing a high C sp (989 F g−1) and a porous shape that serves as an “ion-buffering reservoir” to promote the mobility of electrolyte ions. Similar phenomenon has been observed in our work. In POMo//QDs composites, the presence of pores and tunnels offer better electrode/electrolyte contact, enhanced diffusion, and improved penetration of H+ ions enabling facile charge storage kinetics and maximal usage of reversible redox processes promoting pseudocapacitance [69,70]. Additionally, ionic species can penetrate into the deep realm of the material through the numerous conducting nanochannels that have been possible due to the presence of CQDs and N-CQDs. For all of these reasons, the composites are expected to act as efficient “ion-buffering reservoirs” that have reduced diffusion pathways for the electrolyte ions to permeate effectively [70,71].

The electrochemical supercapacitive activities of POMo and its composites were assessed in a three-electrode system with cyclic voltammetry, GCD and EIS measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4 (aq). All the measurements were conducted at ambient conditions. POMo moieties are highly pseudocapacitive [16,18] indicated by its CV in Fig. 8 (Black). But its current density, hence charge storage capability is not up to the mark. They have redox sites for charge storage, but they lack proper ionic conduction, lower cycle stability, lower SSA, etc., that restricts their practical utilizations [16–20,22,23]. So, to improve its performance, CQDs and N-CQDs were mixed with POMo via solution dispersion method [32] described in the experimental section. Fig. 8A and B illustrate the CVs of the composites of POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs, respectively. It is seen that as we increase the %loading of both CQDs and N-CQDs, the current density, and hence charge storage capabilities of the composites boost markedly. The patterns of CVs of both types of composites, POMo-n% CQDs and POMo-n% N-CQDs, are similar following identical profile. The peaks marked as I (0.35 V), II (0.63 V) (anodic) and I* (0.33 V), II* (0.47 V) (cathodic) in Fig. 8A and B originate from the redox pseudocapacitance of the composites. The fact that the occurrence of the anodic and cathodic peaks at the same potential in both of the composites implies that these peaks are of POMo-derived [18,22,72,73]. POMo is mainly responsible for these redox responses as these are also visible in pristine POMo [22,31]. All of these redox peaks can be assigned to the following rapid reversible electrochemical reactions presented in Eqs. (6), (7) [18,22,45,72,73]. All of the peaks occur at the comparable potentials as in pristine POMo. This bears the evidence that POMo exist in the composite without any severe chemical alterations as seen from previous analyses.

So, we can safely conclude that formations of composites are due to the modifications of some properties on physical grounds leaving POMo unchanged. Some minor peaks are also clearly noticed in both sets of CVs. These are absent for pristine POMo. These minor peaks can be ascribed to intercalation pseudocapacitance [12–14,69–71]. Current density increases appreciably in both cases as the amounts of CQDs and N-CQDs in the composites from 0 to 10 % were increased. But the system miserably disrupts at 15 % QD loading with POMo. We have previously found similar kinds of findings with GO, MnO2, and multi-walled CNTs. With the successive increase (5 %, 10 %, 15 % and so on) in the amount of multi-walled CNTs, the C sp of the composites buildups enormously achieving a high value of 1380 F g−1 for 15 % multi-walled CNTs loading. The composite fails past this 15 % composition [72]. In the present study, for 15 % CQDs or N-CQDs loading, the characteristics peaks in the CVs originated from POMo are missing. A simultaneous contraction in operating potential window with concomitant diminution in current density, similar to the QDs, conveys their inferior supercapacitive performances. CVs of POMo-15 % CQDs and POMo-15 % N-CQDs mimics the CVs of pristine CQDs and N-CQDs (Figs. S2 and 3). This affirm that POMo might have been chemically altered in these cases. Further insights into the capacitive performances were scrutinized with GCD analyses showcased in Fig. 8C and D.

The shapes of the GCD curves in Fig. 8C and D are quasi-triangular depicting that the dominant mechanistic of charge storage in the composites are pseudocapacitive in nature [10,12–14,32,37,38]. Two modes of pseudocapacitance [13,14], intercalation and redox reactions, might have contributed in the charge storage in these composites. As we introduce CQDs and N-CQDs, up to the threshold amount of 10 %, with POMo, the matrix of POMo becomes more porous than before as shown in SEM micrographs in Fig. 7. As a result, more ions can penetrate deep into the matrix of POMo enabling intercalation pseudocapacitance [69,70]. In addition, porous nature and enormous increment of SSA now supports better electrode/electrolyte contact that is conducive for better charge storage [36,38,41]. Moreover, inferred from FTIR analysis in Fig. 1A and EDS analysis in Fig. 6A and B, the O-riched CQDs and N-doped O-riched N-CQDs offer better contact of the composites with the H2SO4 electrolyte making transport of H+ extremely facile [41]. GCDs of POMo-15 % CQDs and POMo-15 % N-CQDs exhibit the same pattern as of pristine CQDs and N-CQDs (Figs. S2 and 3). However, at higher loading the QDs may start to aggregate, which in-turn can reduce the effective surface area available for charge storage, raise internal resistance and impede the ion transport within the composite material [13,15,20,72]. Excessive QDs might also block the active sites of POMo thereby reducing pseudocapacitance [12,13,18,32]. Moreover, structural integrity of the composite might be compromised at higher QDs concentrations as a result of disruption of the optimal POMo-to-QDs ratio. These factors collectively hinder the efficient functions of the composite materials (>15 wt%). Therefore, in the formulation of composite materials for energy storage application, an optimal material-to-material ratio is to be maintained for getting best performance.

The cracked structure of POMo in the composites enable the complete utilization of the active sites of POMo available for redox reactions, hence more charging of the electrode has become possible in the composites [36,38,41]. Moreover, the carbon-based interconnected conductive networks, imparted by the presence of CQDs and N-CQDs in the composites, act as the ion transport channels ensuring deep branch penetration of ions in POMo during charging [21–31]. Nevertheless, CQDs and N-CQDs are themselves pseudocapacitive (Figs. S2 and 3) attributable to the presence of various nitrogenated and oxygenated groups [34,35,38,39,41,43,74] evidenced by the FTIR, EDS and UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis. So, the dispersed CQDs and N-CQDs also contribute to the supercapacitance besides modifying POMo. For all of these reasons, the composites perform better than the pristine POMo. Performance parameters, Table 1, were derived from GCD analysis using Eqs. (1)–(3) [20,42].

POMo exhibited a C sp of only 23 F g−1 at a very low current density of 0.1 A g−1. The calculated values of C sp are even lower at higher current densities (e.g., 19 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1) (Fig. S4, Table S4). Lower C sp of POMo is attributable to several factors such as lower SSA, poor electrical conductivity, surface-limited redox reactions and so on. They exhibit lower cycle rate mainly due agglomeration of POMo moieties [18,19,22,25]. We have achieved a C sp of 397 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1, 21 times greater than POMo, just by adding 5 % CQDs within POMo matrix. Finally, a 35-folds increment of C sp of POMo was achieved with the use of 10 % CQDs with C sp of 660 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1 (Fig. S5, Table S5). The C sp values of CQDs and N-CQDs at 0.2 A g−1 are 33 and 473 F g−1, respectively, indicating that nitrogen doping has improved the charge storage capabilities immensely [35,38,39] (Figs. S2 and 3). So, it is anticipated that the POMo//N-CQDs composite will perform better than the POMo//CQDs composites. It is indeed what happened, revealed by Fig. 8 and Figs. S5 and 6. We have achieved a C sp of 1740 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 with the inclusion of only 5 % N-CQDs. This is ca. 90 times larger than the C sp of pristine POMo. Eventually, at the same current density of 1.0 A g−1, we have secured a C sp of 1796 F g−1 with POMo-10 % N-CQDs composite with enormous energy and power density of 225 W h kg−1 and 475 W kg−1. At a slight lower current density of 0.5 A g−1, this high energy composite has displayed a phenomenal C sp of 1960 F g−1 shown in Table S6, which is nearly 123 times higher than that of the pristine POMo. Effect of current densities on the values of C sp of the prepared composites is presented below (Fig. 9 ).

As we raise the current density, the capacitance value drops and vice versa. The electrical resistance of the electrode grows with higher current density. As a result, at greater current densities, ions fail to make sufficient contact with the electrode and ion diffusion deep into the pores of the material fails [75,76]. Moreover, C sp reduces as a consequence of not having enough time to completely charge up the material. Just the electrode's outer surface contributes to overall capacitance resulting in lower C sp values at higher current densities. With smaller current densities, on the other hand, ions may readily delve deep into the material and occupy all the pores and branches. They can effectively establish contact with the electrode and hence ion penetration is quite high because ions have enough time to accumulate [20,27,32,75,76]. Hence, the overall capacitance is a result of both the outer and inner surfaces. So, we achieve greater C sp values at lower current densities because of this phenomenon.

EIS measurements were conducted to analysis the resistive, capacitive and ion diffusion phenomenon [24,27,36,37,50,77,78] for all the binary composites along with the pristine CQDs, N-CQDs and POMo in the frequency range of 1.0 Hz to 1.0 MHz presented in Fig. 10 . Equivalent series resistance (ESR) and charge transfer resistance (R CT) were calculated from the Nyquist plots. The sum of ESR and R CT for POMo is nearly 40 Ω with appreciable R CT while ESR for CQDs is 2.8 Ω with R CT = 0 Ω indicating facile charge transfer [24,27]. In order to increase the electrical conductivity of POMo, CQDs was added to it. With the addition of 5 % CQDs, the sum of ESR and R CT of POMo drops to 22 Ω with little R CT. Successive addition of CQDs with POMo with 7.5, 10 and 15 % loading results in zero R CT with smaller ESR values. The POMo-10 % CQDs depicts a large reduction in ESR value of POMo from 40 to 4.7 Ω with zero R CT. Similar effects were observed for N-CQDs on POMo (Fig. 10B). The ESR value for N-CQDs is 1.4 Ω, smaller than CQDs, resulted due to the presence of nitrogen atoms. Hence, we can conclude that nitrogen doping successfully reduced ESR and promotes electrical conductivity. R CT for all the composites of POMo and N-CQDs are zero and ESR values for POMo-5 % N-CQDs and POMo-10 % N-CQDs are 4.0 and 2.8 Ω, respectively (inset). Some fitted circuits for our system are presented in Fig. S7.

This diminution of ESR and R CT as CQDs and N-CQDs are added, demonstrates facile charge transfer with faster and quicker ionic transport than before. The embedded QDs generates an interconnected conductive network throughout the matrix of POMo which supports easy movement of ions throughout the composite [36,37]. Hence, better storage of charges is possible explaining the cause of superior supercapacitive performances of the composites. Diminishment of R CT in the composites indicates quicker charge transfer between the electrode/electrolyte interface promoting improved capacitance [37,77]. A vertical line close to the imaginary axis in the lower frequency region reveals better capacitive properties with lower ion diffusional resistance, i.e., lower Warburg resistance (R W) [24,37,50]. The composites delivered smaller R W values exhibiting more stepper, vertical, diagonal lines close to the imaginary axis. This depicts better diffusion of ions into the deep region of the material [36,37,79]. Better ionic transport and deep penetration is possible when the material itself is porous and have a defected, cracked structure [36,70,79]. Similarly, Wei et al. [36] incorporated CQDs with Co3O4 and studied the supercapacitive performances with various electrochemical techniques including EIS. The CQDs/Co3O4 composite demonstrated smaller semicircle in the high frequency region and a more-steeper line toward the imaginary axis in the low frequency region indicating that the presence of CQDs offers quicker charge transfer kinetics along with better ion diffusion. Again, Wang et al. [37] studied the effects of CQDs on the electrochemical supercapacitive performances of NiCo2O4 with EIS analysis. They found a more vertical line at the low frequency region displaying easier diffusion of electrolyte ions for the CQDs/NiCo2O4 composite. Furthermore, ESR and R CT values were found to be lesser for CQDs/NiCo2O4 than NiCo2O4 just as were found for POMo//QDs composites. Thus, the incorporation of QDs promotes improved ionic conductivities that in turn promotes efficient H+ storage. Similarly, RuO2 decorated with reduced CQDs displayed better ionic motion, smaller contact resistance, facile diffusion of electrolyte ions, high power performance inferred from their EIS analysis [77].

SEM micrographs in Fig. 7 agrees with this statement revealing the porous nature of the composites. Again, the presence of O and N, revealed from FTIR, EDS and UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis, in the composite enables better contact between the electrode and electrolyte [41]. The presence of carbon-based QDs endow the composites with improved electrical conductivities (EIS analysis in Fig. 10A and B) along with a larger SSA and porous structure (Fig. 7). Presence of QDs also enable the full exposure of the active sites of POMo. All of these alterations promote better storage of ions with easier movement of H+ even into the inner portion of the material.

The bode plot [36,41], which depicts the dependency of phase angle on frequency, is shown in Fig. 10C. The frequency response of the supercapacitors may be evaluated by comparing the characteristic frequency (
f 0
), which is the frequency at a phase angle of −45° or with its associated relaxation time constant (
τ 0
=
1 /
f 0
). At
f 0
, the supercapacitor converts into purely capacitive from purely resistive form [41]. The device can discharge all of its energy with an efficiency >50 % in the smallest amount of time, which is the relaxation time constant (
τ 0
). The shortened relaxation time constant offers additional evidence for high-speed ion diffusion and transport activities [36,41].
f 0
values for POMo, POMo-10 % CQDs and POMo-10 % N-CQDs are 32, 56 and 67 Hz, respectively, corresponding to 31.25, 17.86 and 14.93 ms
τ 0
values. This further indicates quicker diffusion of ions and high-speed transport behavior promoting faster charge-discharge rate of the composites [41,78]. Zhang et al. [78] showed that graphene quantum dots displayed a
τ 0
of 0.28 s at −45° whereas reduced graphene oxide exhibited 0.55 s. Oxygen and nitrogen rich carbon nanosheets demonstrated a
τ 0
of 11.23 s [41]. Hence, our composites are more efficient for the application of high energy density supercapacitor. Ragone plot in Fig. 10D signifies the supremacy of this work. Our materials demonstrate energy density closer to that of batteries and power density closer to that of parallel-plate capacitors.

Cycle stability of POMo-5 % N-CQDs and POMo-10 % N-CQDs were checked for 3000 cycles at a very ultra-high current density of 20 A g−1 (Fig. 11 ). It has been found that both of them demonstrated sticking tolerance toward prolonged charging-discharging cycles expressing impressive cycle lifespan. Even at this extreme current density, POMo-5 % N-CQDs and POMo-10 % N-CQDs retained 92 and 96 % of their starting capacitance, respectively. The engineered materials exhibit resilience even at extreme current densities, enabling them to effortlessly endure cycles exceeding 3000 without compromising performance. CVs and GCDs of the composites of 1st cycle and after 3000 charging-discharging cycles are given in Figs. S8 and 9. This kind of extraordinary cycle-stability depicts that the structures are highly robust manifesting the toughening ability of the QDs [42]. The tiny, inconsequential decrease in charge storage capabilities originated due to the deactivation or blockage of some active sites [74]. Moreover, some ups and downs in capacitance retention are seen in both of plots. This type of behavior is typically caused by simultaneous activation and deactivation of active sites. The pores of the materials may have collapsed as a result of constant charging-discharging rendering the pores unusable for charge storage. Likewise, a loss in surface area with less contact with the electrolytes may be blamed [80,81]. With continuous charging-discharging cycles, these issues were automatically resolved and the system again got back its previous pace. The supercapacitor behaves as though it is a 'self-healing supercapacitor' capable of restoring its prior performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical kinetics and deconvolution of C sp
ce:section.ce:para: The Trasatti and Dunn plots are used to determine the amount of charge stored at the surface of an active material as well as the principles involved [82–85]. In POMo//QDs-based materials, charge storage mainly stems from EDLC and redox reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface [15,16,18]. Moreover, H+ ions from the H2SO4 electrolyte diffuse simultaneously within the deep realm and undergo intercalation/deintercalation onto the active electrode surface of POMo//QDs composites giving rise to some extent of charge storage [86]. To understand the mechanisms involved and to deconvolute the percentage of charge storage contribution, Trasatti and Dunn methods have been employed [83–85].

The surface- and diffusion-controlled capacitance behavior influences the overall rate performance and cycle stability of an electrode material. In order to get a quantitative insight into the charge storage kinetics and capacitive behavior of the POMo//QDs composites, we have further analyzed the CV curves measured at 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04, and 0.05 V s−1 (Fig. S6) for POMo-10 % N-CQDs. The anodic/cathodic peaks shift slightly while the general profile remains identical, demonstrating that the POMo//QDs composites have excellent electrochemical reversibility [84]. The mathematical relationship between peak current (i) and scan rate (v) is described by the following equations [83–85]:
(8)
i = a
v b
(9)
log i = b log v + log a
where a and b are two arbitrary variables. Here, b is an important parameter that signifies the mode of charge storage processes, capacitive and diffusion-controlled, involved [84]. b = 1 signifies the surface capacitive behavior (EDLC, pseudocapacitance) and b = 0.5 indicates that the charging-discharging steps are controlled by the ionic diffusion process like the faradaic intercalation process [85]. The value of b can be easily obtained from the slope of the log i vs log v plot shown in Fig. 12A. For both anodic and cathodic processes, b has been found to be 0.73 and 0.85, respectively. Any value within 0 to 1 implies that both faradaic and non-faradaic processes operate in the functioning of the system [84,85]. POMo//QDs-based electrochemical system exhibits a hybrid behavior with capacitive dominance indicative from the b values that are approximately close to 1. Afterwards, the capacitive and diffusion-controlled influences have been measured using the following set of equations [83–85].

(10), a bar plot (Fig. 12C) has been constructed depicting diffusion-controlled and capacitive contributions [84,85]. It has been uncovered that the capacitive contribution rate increases with scan rates that takes up significant portion of the total charge while diffusive contribution reduces. The reason for the increase as well as the decrease is the limitation of intercalated pseudocapacitance onto the electrode surface and the lack of time for the H+ ion diffusion at elevated scan rates [84].

To further investigate the significance of electrode structure on total capacitance, we have applied Trasatti's approach to deconvolute the C sp into electrode's outer and inner surfaces contributions [83,85]. The charge generated by the EDL process and pseudocapacitance can be estimated using this approach. The inner layer charge, pseudocapacitance, is usually obtained by deducting the EDL contribution (outer layer charge) from the overall capacitance [85,87].

Fig. 13A displays the plot of 1/C sp vs. v 1/2 from which the total volumetric C sp (F g−1) can be determined by linear fitting extrapolation to figure out the intercept of the plot. The intercept at extremely sluggish scan rate gives the magnitude of (1/C sp)Total, which is 0.00054 in this case. Hence, (C sp)Total has been found to be 1852 F g−1 which is nearly close to the C sp (F g−1) obtained from the current density analysis (Fig. 8) for POMo-10 % N-CQDs. After that, the capacitance contribution originating from the outer surfaces (EDLC) has been calculated from the C sp vs. v −1/2 plot presented in Fig. 13B. The value of the intercept, (C sp)outer, has been found to be 843 F g−1. Thus, the pseudocapacitive contribution toward H+ storage at the inner surface of POMo-10 % N-CQDs is (C sp)Inner = 1009 F g−1 obtained by deducting (C sp)outer from (C sp)Total [83,85,87]. Hence, the EDLC (outer surface) and pseudocapacitance (inner surface) are 45.5 and 54.5 %, respectively, (Fig. 13C). CQDs and N-CQDs mainly contribute to EDLC and POMo dictate the pseudocapacitance, while their combined actions result in enhanced electrochemical performance observed in the POMo//CQDs and POMo//N-CQDs composites.

Electrochemical capacitors store energy usually in the form of EDL at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Every electrified electrode dipped in an electrolyte solution generates a unique type of interfacial area known as the electric double layer. With the creation of this stiff double layer, charge buildup increases, i.e., C sp increases [7,8,11]. C sp, on the other hand, is greatly affected by the faradaic pseudocapacitance originated by the deposited material on the electrode, as previously mentioned [12–14]. In this work, we have used POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs. POMo stores charges mainly by dint of pseudocapacitance [18,22,45,72,73] (see Figure S4). On the other hand, CQDs and N-CQDs mainly accumulate charges via the formation of EDL but they exhibit pseudocapacitance to some extent too [34] (Figs. S2 and 3).

From FTIR and UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis, we decipher that CQDs and N-CQDs are highly functionalized with various oxygenated and nitrogenated organic groups. Existence of these organic groups in the periphery of the QDs induces redox pseudocapacitance [34,38–41]. The petty performance of POMo was boosted by adding QDs with it. The QDs are embedded on the matrix of POMo as pictorially shown in Fig. 14A. This increases its porous nature promoting intercalation pseudocapacitance and higher surface area [36,38,41,70] inferred from its SEM analysis. Moreover, the EIS analysis have showed that the composites have better electrical conductance properties, rendered by the QDs, compared to pristine POMo facilitating faster electronic/ionic transportation throughout the composites [22,36,37,77]. TEM analysis reveals that the average particle size of CQDs is 4.9 nm with 0.25 nm crystallite size (XRD analysis, Table 1). Doping raised the crystallite size of N-CQDs to 0.73 nm [61,68]. As a result, we might speculate that the particle size of N-CQDs is somewhat larger but still near to 4.9 nm. POMo, on the other hand, has a considerably high crystallite size compared to QDs, with a value of 53.7 nm (Table 1). The QDs get dispersed uniformly and homogeneously under the influence of ultra-sonication in the POMo matrix triggering several modifications on physical grounds involving surface area, porosity, and electrical conductivity.

Hence, the composites benefit from the full exposure of the redox active sites of POMo. As a result, not only surface POMo but also bulk POMo take part in charge storage as seen via Dunn and Trasatti analysis. Presence of carbon-based QDs make the movement of ions facile thoughout the material as their presence generate ion transporting channels in the POMo matrix as demonstrated by the EIS analysis. QDs themselves also contribute to charge storage due to the presence of surface functional groups [34,41]. The probable reversible, rapid, redox reactions including Eqs. (6), (7) that may contribute to pseudocapacitive charge storage in our supercapacitor electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution are expressed by Eqs. (12)–(15).

A presumptive charge storage diagram is given below in Fig. 14B. In POMo, the active sites are not available for fully for redox pseudocapacitance. Lack of surface area and pores, proper electrical conductivity, concealment of active sites, agglomeration of POMo, less contact with the electrolyte and so on render it as an ineffective candidate for supercapacitor application [16–20,22,23]. All of these issues were checked using CQDs and N-CQDs and also, they added new sites for charge storage. CQDs and N-CQDs, in fact, introduce newer dimensions to the capabilities of POMo as a high-performance electrode material. The overall electrochemical charge storage performances of the studied composites can be further improved by introducing other heteroatoms like sulphur, boron, phosphorus, and so on. Ensuring homogeneous fabrication of the composites over the surface of the electrode and reducing its active mass further can also improve the C sp. The composites based on POMo with CQDs and N-CQDs seem to result in enhanced charge storage.

In this work, we have developed a novel electrode material by embedding CQDs and N-CQDs on the matrix of POMo to boost its supercapacitive performance. The surface of CQDs and N-CQDs, synthesized from lemon juice through hydrothermal carbonization, are extensively functionalized with a multitude of oxygenated and nitrogenated groups. This extensive functionalization enhances wettability, promoting improved contact between the electrode and electrolyte while contributing to redox pseudocapacitance. The embedment of the quantum dots into the POMo matrix, creates a porous, open, defective structure with ion-conducting channels allowing protonic migration to the active sites of POMo that were previously unavailable. This facilitates enhanced ion permeation and increases the available surface area of POMo for charge storage. Furthermore, they strengthen and reinforce the POMo structure, averting its dissolution and agglomeration, resulting in extended cycle lifespans for the robust binary composites. The as-formed composites exhibit improved supercapacitive and rate capabilities compared to pristine POMo demonstrating C sp of 660 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1 for POMo-10 % CQDs and 1960 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 for POMo-10 % N-CQDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 (aq) with roughly 96 % retention of original C sp after 3000 charging-discharging cycles at 20.0 A g−1 depicting their robust nature. This indicates that N-CQDs-based materials outperform CQDs-based materials, showcasing the effectiveness of nitrogen doping. Our work provides a new insight and opens up a new pathway to explore the energy storage behavior of polyoxometalates and lemon-derived nitrogen doped CQDs, which may be used as a general strategy for formulating high energy density supercapacitors.
